i have two major points that i think we should address, either in this PR or as part of an immediate refactoring:
1. processing notifications asynchronously
2. i think the event notification code would be easier to understand and more maintainable if we used OOP concepts and introduced some basic abstractions. in particular to process event notifications asynchronously it will help to encapsulate the event context into an object, and homogenize the send_event api. i made some suggestions inline.

i think 1. is quite essential since that seems to be how AWS behaves. at least in the lambda documentation it says that [Amazon S3 invokes your function asynchronously](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/with-s3.html).

in terms of code organization i think we should move all the code related to notifications into a separate module `notifications.py` to keep the `provider.py` a little slimmer.

@bentsku let's discuss who will do the refactoring.

This is a pretty good usecase to test whether it's actually necessary to have both `pyo3` and `rust-cpython` types in existence at the same time. In general, I don't think that it is: see below.

----

Taking [ensure_remote_has_recursive](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/b4febf1c392aa05269af782401366e030e38a6e3/src/rust/engine/src/externs/interface.rs#L1648-L1676) as an example: the signature takes a `PyList` containing generic `PyObject` instances, each of which we assert is either a `PyDigest` or a `PyFileDigest` instance: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/b4febf1c392aa05269af782401366e030e38a6e3/src/rust/engine/src/externs/interface.rs#L1661-L1662

Both `rust-cpython` and `pyo3` have unsafe methods that take a `PyObject` pointer:
* https://dgrunwald.github.io/rust-cpython/doc/cpython/struct.PyObject.html#method.from_owned_ptr
* https://docs.rs/pyo3/0.14.1/pyo3/prelude/struct.Py.html#method.from_owned_ptr

And that means that we can convert from the `PyObject` type of one of them to the `PyObject` type of the other using a small amount of unsafe code. Once you've converted from one `PyObject` type into the other, you can use the per-library casting methods to convert into the type that you actually wanted.

----

So: it seems like it should be fine for a `rust-cpython` method to accept types generated by `pyo3`, and vice-versa. The primary downside is that you must accept `PyObject` rather than a more specific type in the interface (but the `mypy` typestubs would still be accurate, so the majority of our type safety would be preserved).

Hi @HorlogeSkynet , maybe I'm misunderstanding something, but right now I only see the part that prevents "unjust" escapes from the chroot (from absolute links or from going above the root) but I don't see any code that would "bend" "legit" absolute symlinks back into the chroot.

Let me demo what I think we'd need in some form:

```console
def resolve_chroot_symlink(link_location, root_dir):
    assert os.path.commonprefix([link_location, root_dir]) == root_dir

    while True:
        try:
            resolved = os.readlink(link_location)
        except FileNotFoundError:  # includes case "not a symlink"
            return link_location

        if resolved.startswith('/'):
            # i.e. absolute path (with regard to the chroot)
            # that we need to move back inside the chroot
            resolved = os.path.join(root_dir, resolved.lstrip('/'))
        else:
            # i.e. relative path that we make absolute
            resolved = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(link_location), resolved)

        if os.path.commonprefix([resolved, root_dir]) != root_dir:
            raise Value('ESCAPE DETECTED')  # TODO

        link_location = resolved
```

What do you think?

There are some minor changes needed, but we should also follow up on a couple of these scopes -- as noted below -- to make sure they are good to include.

---

The `<X>Scopes.<scope_name>` interface is pretty easy to use, and should be easy to document. It might be good to add a page to the docs for this, to ensure that these functions and constants are included. We can do that in a follow-up if you prefer.

One thing which is missing is that we need to decide on where these imports should be done from for users. Some options:
- `src/globus_sdk/__init__.py` --> `from globus_sdk import urn_scope_string`
- `src/globus_sdk/scopes.py` --> `from globus_sdk.scopes import urn_scope_string`

I think I prefer the latter, but both are fine.

> I didn't want to get clever with dynamically assigning attributes on the `XScopes` objects.

I think keeping it simple for a first cut is good, but I did raise the possibility of a `ScopeBuilder` or `ResourceServerScopes` object below. My reasoning centers more around dynamic cases, like GCS scopes, for which some kind of object to represent "the scopes for a resource server" might make sense as a model.

Another way to look at it is that if the interface for `AuthScopes` and `TransferScopes` is good, it may be nice to allow users to build a similar object dynamically, as in `MyGCSScopes = Foo(<id>)`.

Let's discuss this at our next meeting. The problem currently is that there are still a lot of necessary Python calls in the loop (e.g., `Stimulus`, `BiphasicPulse`), so you can't release the gil. This would explain why the speedup is limited... 

Making this work is tricky. You'd have to strip all the Python overhead and e.g. purely operate on arrays. However, currently the array changes shape on each iteration, which is also slow.

One way might be to:

1.  Do all the Python/error checks outside the Cython function
2.  Use p2p to create `BiphasicPulse` outside Cython. Then only pass the time & data arrays of the `BiphasicPulse` object
3.  Preallocate the time & data arrays of the final `Stimulus` that will be created
4.  Fill the values of the arrays in the loop without calling out to Python objects

Hi @pennersr, thanks for the PR (and thanks for your work on allauth!).

Beyond the inline change requests (really just a nicer syntax (and faster execution) for shallow-copying dicts):

* Should this have increased test coverage?
* I see `pre_login` is a very new feature (https://github.com/pennersr/django-allauth/commit/fde96e6e7da6bbfd9faffaa90d75e6c3e7c2cc41) only released in 0.48.0 (14 hours young at the time of writing) so as-is, this makes django-allauth-2fa incompatible with older versions of django-allauth. The required minimum version in `setup.cfg` also needs to be changed to `>=0.48.0`. That, in turn, would make this a semver-majoresque change. That doesn't mean this won't get merged – I like the simplifications (similar to the stuff I'd done in #76) but it likely won't make it to the next point release.

It's hard for me to posit from above Windows-based failure that the above indicated misplacement. Can I ask if the following is the correct reasoning, reasoning within Python 3.6?

- Calling `PyEval_InitThreads()` in `get_internals()` via nominal usage did nothing per [3.6 docs](https://docs.python.org/3.6/c-api/init.html#c.PyEval_InitThreads) stating that "This is a no-op when called for a second time", so this is a harmless (but noisy) misplacement.
- The missing call in `embed.h` are why Windows (and others?) fail. This smells like it'd happen when the embedded interpreter exits before `get_internals()` was called. Is this the case? (is it spurious, or deterministic?)

From reading https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/issues/10798 it sounds like we could potentially better address this by incrementally dirtying, rather than skipping dirtying entirely... I'll outline why I'm concerned about the approach in this PR, and then describe how I think incremental dirtying could look in that example...

Reduced example (and wee had some of this discussion on https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/pull/9015 :)):

`IsSameAsGoldenFile` reads a source file, and compares it with the copy of that source file on a branch whose name was specified in another file:

```
                                    SomeGoal
                                       |
                                  SomeOtherRule
                                       |
                                IsSameAsGoldenFile
                                  /            \   
ReadFileFromSourceTree("path/in/repo")   ReadFileFromBranch("path/in/repo", output_of(dep))
                                                             |   
                             GetGitShaFromBranchName("path/of/name/of/golden/branch", uncacheable=true)
```

Let's say `IsSameAsGoldenFile` has already executed in this run, because it has pleasantly few dependencies.

Then let's say `path/in/repo` changes in the source tree (say, because it was changed by formatter).

With this PR as it stands, I believe the semantics are to say "We already ran `IsSameAsGoldenFile` in this build, because it happens to have a transitive dep on `GetGitShaFromBranchName` we won't re-run it, even though that's unrelated to the reason we _should_ dirty it". So we're going to return a stale result for `IsSameAsGoldenFile`.

In general, I think this is a symptom of the semantics of relying on re-running nodes in response to filesystem change during a build. But if we're signed up to those semantics, I think we need to honour them in this situation. While "You wrote an uncacheable node, so you get different semantics for that node" feels reasonable for a rule author, "You happen to transitively depend on an uncacheable node in another ruleset which you don't know about, so you get different semantics" feels like it will be very surprising to both rule-authors and users.

I _think_ what this PR is trying to avoid is having to (potentially repeatedly) traverse up the tree to mark `SomeOtherRule` and `SomeGoal` as dirty, and then, if `IsSameAsGoldenFile` happened to be the same result, to re-mark them as clean - it's not intentionally trying to avoid re-evaluating nodes which actually need to be re-evaluated, that's just an accidental side-effect of trying to do all the dirty/clean book-keeping (have we observed this to be a problem, by the way? Or is this just defensive?).

Something I think we could do differently to achieve the same aim (and I haven't thought this through much), is to not eagerly transitively dirty things. So when `ReadFileFromSourceTree` is invalidated, it _only_ dirties `IsSameAsGoldenFile`. Then when we re-evaluate `IsSameAsGoldenFile`, if the result is the same, we're done, but if the result is different, we'd then dirty `SomeOtherRule`, and so on. This may cause us to perform work we're going to throw away (e.g. if we evaluate `SomeOtherRule` thinking `IsSameAsGoldenFile` is clean, but then find out it's dirty so throw away its result), so it may suggest we'd want to de-prioritise the potentially-dirty call-stacks if there are other call-stacks we could be evaluating, but eventually the dirtying will propagate up far enough that we should never _finish_ with stale results.

According to the ST7735 documentation, a reset is triggered if RESX is held low longer than 5us and can take up to 120ms to complete depending on the power state of the display (see section 9.16 in [ST7735 data sheet](https://www.displayfuture.com/Display/datasheet/controller/ST7735.pdf))  If this were a display specific setting (as opposed to the interface), I'd recommend making the default something like 150ms.  Since it is not, this seems like a reasonable approach.  

FYI, it is possible that the problem is not how long you held the pin low as I doubt that you can toggle the pin from high-low-high in under 5us using RPi.GPIO. I suspect instead it was beginning to transmit data before the reset was completed.  This issue of certain commands (like reset) taking longer than other commands is a problem I was attempting to solve by introducing an optional exec_time in parallel_device so that depending on the command being issued, an appropriate delay to wait for the command to finish could be specified.  Just something to think about...

Worse still, for my justification, is that this is an easy PR to review. I just read through all of the changes and it looks great. I can see that it's primarily a URI translation: the `s3://` URI scheme can be rewritten as an HTTPS scheme, and then the `HTTPSource` can take over.

I thought there was an issue with getting arguments from `uproot.open` into the `S3Source` constructor, but then I answered my own question: it goes through `**self._options`:

https://github.com/scikit-hep/uproot5/blob/a8644df7a96ee2d788adb1e35f326ba160bedf48/src/uproot/reading.py#L578-L583

Have you tested that? (With both `uproot.open` and another, like `uproot.iterate`?)

It can be hard to write a permanent CI test for the testing suite without having a file that won't ever be deleted or moved—worse still if it requires AWS secrets to authenticate. Would this work?

```
s3://pivarski-princeton/chep-2021-jagged-jagged-jagged/zlib9-rntuple-jagged0.root
```

It's publicly accessible and it won't be going away because it's used in so many big-data tests. You'd have to set `entry_stop=100` if you try to read from a TTree, though I don't see a need to do that (accessing just the metadata object should be enough to show that S3 is working). Also, I own it, and I'm easy to get in touch with if it ever does go away.

I should also let you know that we plan to revive #692, which should eventually run all backends through fsspec, and that would include S3. But it's better to add this feature now and have it be useful before the long-term plan becomes a reality.

Some process oriented feedback:
I'm finding this PR a challenge to review because of its multifaceted nature. This PR includes renames, type annotations, and serdes changes - I think with renames and type annotations we can generally trust our CICD to catch most things, and so I think review on those individual pieces would be quick. Serdes changes however, even just a simple rename, I generally just don't feel super confident in our tests catching everything. I'm not convinced that all of the serdes objects are under test well enough to ensure that backcompat is always preserved. I think the amalgam of these three things leaves me feeling unconfident in relying on our CICD to catch any errors that might exist - not to mention typos, rebase artifacts etc. All in all, I do actually feel the need to comb every file of this PR, which is taking a while.

I think I understand why this PR is as big as it is, this is certainly a large undertaking. However for future rename suites (I assume a solid one is coming down the pipe) I'd have the following suggestions so that we can have reviews happen quicker:
- Separate out any serdes renames into their own PR, and maybe mark in github via comments where those renames are occurring so that I can easily find them.
- Don't include type annotations - I understand that it's net good for the codebase, but I just find it to muddle things when scanning through

In any case, great job on getting this done - just some suggestions for streamlining review of stuff like this in the future

Don't feel that strongly about this though, if having PCL treat null the same as empty is vastly easier here that doesn't seem dangerous.

@ArturNiederfahrenhorst I just did a more thorough pass on your LSTM tests. The test is not really testing the rollout of LSTM cells during training (test_forward_train)

What you are doing right now:
- stacking obs, actions, and states in multiple BATCHES. Where is the time dimension then?
- This is like using LSTM cells as a fully connected cell :) 

What you should be doing:
- stack obs, and actions in both batches and times. So that the final shape of batch is (b, t, ho) and the final shape of actions is (b, t, ha)
- stack the state_in to be of shape (b, n_layer=1, hs) 
- if the number of steps are not compatible with (b, t) reshaping you need to zero pad. For example if you took 30 steps and your max_rollout_len = 20. You need to create a (1, 20, ho) obs tensor and concat if with a (1, 10, ho) obs. You cannot. So you need to zero pad the second one to make it (1, 20, ho) and take care of zeros with masking. In this example your state_in shape should be (2, 1, hs). 

Makes sense?
I would recommend taking out the lstm stuff in forward_train from your test. your rollout test seems correct. 

Do the lstm forward_train in a separate method and PR.

I worrying about only one thing. After this PR port `12400` is treated as "not set". This is not true from the user point of view. Imagine the following scenario. One reads LightGBM docs and finds that port for distributed training can be set via [`local_listen_port`](https://lightgbm.readthedocs.io/en/latest/Parameters.html#local_listen_port) parameter and its default value is `12400`. User doesn't have any preferences for ports and decides to leave default value (or more critical problem: harcodes `12400` in params). The user goes to firewall setting and opens `12400` port with the hope that everything will work. But it won't work because actually **random**, not `12400`, port will be used inside LightGBM.

Things are getting worse due to that there is no dedicated `port` param in Dask wrapper that can be set to say `None` and take priority over `local_listen_port` is kwargs. I have only one solution for this inconsistency for now:
document in Dask docs that actually **only for Dask** port `12400 === random`.

My biggest concern about this change is moving the hack into the setuptools namespace. I would have liked to have this package in some shared namespace, but the fact of the matter is:

- importing setuptools invokes a lot of behavior, including [monkeypatching distutils](https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/db37ab088bc826bdd407b141d68e4aab13771dda/setuptools/__init__.py#L242).
- importing setuptools imports pkg_resources, which invokes a lot more behavior and is known to be slow.

It's simply not acceptable to force every environment to import setuptools on startup. Therefore, the shim needs not to import pkg_resources or setuptools in order to install the shim. The import of either should be deferred until distutils is imported.

For my understanding, in the flow from the network through Plasma to building the DataFrame, where was the large memory inflation happening? My understanding of the flow is:
1. Plasma buffers are allocated and filled as objects are pulled, with the Arrow tables being deserialized directly into the Plasma buffers.
2. The block builder holds pointers to those Plasma buffers, so no new copies are made while add blocks to the builder.
3. The `.build()` and `.to_pandas()` calls create a copy or two during the Arrow table concatenation and the DataFrame construction, respectively.

This PR deals with stage (1) and (2) rather than the copy-heavy stage (3), and AFAICT wouldn't result in a lower peak during those stages. Am I missing the creation of an extra copy or two in (1) and (2), or something else?

sorry based on offline conversation with @fubuloubu -- @pandadefi since point of this PR is just to make it syntactically simpler to call ecrecover with an existing uint8 (without having to `convert()`) but the ecrecover precompile just expects 32 bytes, it would introduce inefficiency for use cases where the user has a `uint256` (since those are always checked to be in range for v anyways).

to address this, i think we should make ecrecover accept both kinds of input. in other words, `v` can be in `(uint8, uint256)` and r and s in `(bytes32, uint256)`. the only downside is that it makes the API more confusing, but there is no EVM-level reason to pick one of these types over another.

One requirement noted in #10240 is that we expect users' selections to be retained as they go back through the forms.

With this implementation, we may need to also maintain the values of particular pieces of context, conditionally.

For example on the first page - the default selection is "On my own". If you click "Group learning" -> Continue -> Go back then the default selection is shown, rather than what the user selected originally.

This isn't a huge UX issue (IMO) for that particular example -- but once the user gets into the "Full Device Creation" flow, they'll be going through the handful of questions and if they go back to re-read something then they shouldn't have their selections reset.

The complication comes in because, particularly in the "BACK_SKIP_FACILITY_FORM" event where we run the `revertFullDeviceImport`, we are resetting the selected device and facility. These context properties are used across multiple flows -- so if a user selects a device and facility to import from for a _full device_ - but realizes they really meant to do _learn only_ - then their previous selection would be automatically kept.

Now that I write that out, it actually seems like it might be best not to clear it out because why would they select the wrong device+facility in one flow, go to the other and do a different flow?

---

In any case, it seems that whether we use history and just pop back to the last one, we then will have to manually maintain the context -- except in the specific cases where we deem it necessary to just go back to where we were context and all (ie, if we want to clear out the user's selected devices when they go back from an import without having already imported anything).

I wonder if we could just have `BACK` and something like `BACK_WITH_CONTEXT` where the first does what it does here, then the latter could apply the context of the _current_ state to the target state's context. 

The pattern I used whereby I only set the context whenever the user clicks "continue" means that the previous state will always nullify the context that the transition to the current state set a value to. So one way or the other, we need to be able to maintain previous context when going back.

Based on the findings, I would just change the commit message / PR description to something like this then:

```
Fix for #89, List Items separated by tab character not parsed correctly.

Also fixes failing examples 312 and 313 in the CommonMark 0.30 spec due to the way leading space is now checked for list items.

The 1st problem was that `# check if next_line starts List` considered only markers followed
by a space and not by a tab. Another problem was that the implemented tab expansion
to tab stops (of width 4 as per spec) could work only in a limited scope.

This fix uses `expandtabs()` to implement the tab stops correctly and moves extraction
of content into the `parse_marker()` and `parse_continuation()` methods. This lets
us implement use cases like "list interrupts a paragraph" and "list item continuation"
in a less error-prone way.
```

> > Also, how do you include it in a `sdist` if it's outside?
> 
> This is the interesting part, if you review my code changes and description in the issue. It isn't copying the file into the project directory, it is copy the content and put in some place, in this case in the `PKG-INFO` file.


I think you may have missed the point. If your project loads its long_description from `../README`, then you build an sdist and ship that off to PyPI, and then I download the sdist and try to build it, it will fail to build because ../README doesn't exist.

What's worse, though, is if you use `long_description = file:/etc/passwd`, and then you build an sdist and ship that off to your colleague who builds a wheel on their build server and installs that to their applications, now `importlib.metadata.metadata('your-package')['Description']` will have the contents of `/etc/passwd` for the build server.

I'm sorry, but it's not about security, but it's about having hermetic packages whose sources are transportable.

Is my understanding correct that `yield from dbt.cli(["run"])` doesn't yet work?

My only remaining reservation about this API is about that, and I'm wondering whether this is the right place to give feedback or somewhere else is better.

TLDR my reservation is that I think that users might find the behavior of a generator returned by `dbt.cli` unintuitive:
- The first time that `next` is invoked on the generator, it will invoke the dbt CLI to run all the dbt models, but just return an `AssetMaterialization` that corresponds to one of the models.
- Subsequent invocations of `next` won't impact what dbt models get run, they'll just return `AssetMaterialization`s.

I think that an API that somehow distinguishes between "kicking off computations" and "streaming back the results" might be easier to understand and harder to misuse.  Haven't thought about the names much, but maybe something like:

```python
yield from dbt.cli(...).stream_results()
```

Everything else in this PR looks good to me, although I will admit to not being an expert on the guts of the dbt integration.

Hmm I feel like it is not a good idea to query all drivers. This pattern has not been working well in the past too for RPC that require reliable response time. There are 3 cons; 

- it introduces a new code path when we already have an aggregation path everyone knows. 
- I believe GetAllJobInfo is called frequently which can add unnecessary loads to user drivers.
- It can be common for the driver to be overloaded sometimes. In this case, GetAllJobInfo APIs will become extremely slow. If 1 driver is slow to respond. If the driver exits unexpectedly, it can take up to keepalive timeout (30~60 seconds) to detect the failure.

Thanks, Vlad, for this significant contribution!  Embracing the cuDNN v8 API, as this PR does for the Convolution and Deconvolution operator, is an important step forward for MXNet.

The v8 API treats operations generically, where the same function calls are used for creating, plan-finalizing, and executing ops.  This is inherently more scalable and supportable as the library of ops grows.  Also with the cuDNN v8 API comes a more powerful representation of convolution implementations- rather than being one of a handful of choices identified by an "algo number", the v8 API expands this notion to "engine configs" (a combination of primary engine number and lesser parameters known as "knobs").  This enhanced approach should result in increased performance, particularly for non-classic models.

Users should be aware that with the move to the cuDNN v8 API, the actual conv kernels used may change, although this has always been true of even minor release updates.  In case of an undesirable change in model behavior, this PR provides the hooks (via environment variables) for troubleshooting and filtering out certain conv engines (based on numerical notes or engine id).

And finally, I'm excited that with this PR MXNet now 'owns' the convolution autotuning process.  That should allow future PRs to improve on the determinism of the autotuning result.  This PR introduces an autotuner that evaluates a conv engine config by the average of 3 timings.  Should this introduce an unwanted delay in model startup, the user can turn off autotuning with MXNET_CUDNN_AUTOTUNE_DEFAULT=0.  The convolution engine configs would then be chosen via heuristics, which have been improved considerably in the cuDNN v8 release.
